serving.natural_language_portion: One small bowl of capsicum salad (approximately 200 g) is a typical serving as a starter or side dish in a Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sweet red pepper, raw (FDC ID 170108) and mixed vegetable salad components; adapted composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Lao Hu Cai / Chinese capsicum salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for raw sweet red pepper (FDC ID 170108) and raw cucumber, adjusted for the addition of soy sauce (sodium contribution ~100–150 mg/100g), sesame oil (fat contribution), and black vinegar. The input record values (energy 60 kcal, protein 1.5 g, fat 4.0 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g, sodium 325 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol 0.0 mg and trans fat 0.0 g are confirmed zero for this plant-based dish with sesame oil dressing.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on non-starchy mixed vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lao Hu Cai / Chinese capsicum salad. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for non-starchy mixed vegetables and raw capsicum (bell pepper), which consistently fall in the GI range of 10–20. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 10.0) / 100 = 1.5, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 10.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 325 mg per 100 g (650 mg per standard 200 g serving), primarily from soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce and reduce dressing quantity; the potassium content (~220 mg/100 g from capsicum, cucumber, and onion) partially offsets sodium load, but total per-serving sodium (≈650 mg) represents approximately 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit and warrants portion awareness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5 g/100 g), low GI (~15), high vitamin C (40 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Capsicum salad is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, low glycemic index, and meaningful fibre; black vinegar in the dressing has evidence for attenuating post-meal glucose response, and capsicum's high vitamin C supports insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and dominant unsaturated fat from sesame oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lipid profile of this dish is favourable for cardiovascular health — saturated fat is minimal, trans fat and cholesterol are absent, and the primary fat source (sesame oil) provides unsaturated fatty acids; however, sodium at 325 mg/100 g should be moderated by using low-sodium soy sauce to avoid compounding hypertensive cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsicum (bell pepper) and chilli are nightshade vegetables containing solanine alkaloids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid this dish, as capsicum and chilli contain solanine alkaloids that may trigger or worsen joint inflammation in susceptible patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Capsicum Salad (老虎菜, Lao Hu Cai, literally 'Tiger Vegetable') is a traditional Chinese cold salad originating from northern China and widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore as a starter or side dish. The dish features shredded or julienned capsicum (bell peppers, typically red and green), cucumber, onion, and fresh coriander, tossed in a savoury and tangy dressing of soy sauce, black vinegar, sesame oil, garlic, and optionally chilli oil. The preparation is entirely raw and cold — no cooking is applied to the vegetables — making it a crisp, refreshing dish with a bright, pungent flavour profile combining the sweetness of capsicum, the acidity of black vinegar, the nuttiness of sesame oil, and the heat of chilli. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (~60 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (5 g/100 g), and provides meaningful vitamin C (40 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g). The primary fat source is sesame oil, which contributes unsaturated fatty acids and keeps saturated fat very low (0.5 g/100 g). Sodium is moderate at 325 mg/100 g due to soy sauce; low-sodium soy sauce is recommended for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal. It is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate load. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and nightshade sensitivity should avoid this dish due to the capsicum and chilli content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Lao Hu Cai exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for raw capsicum and cucumber components, adjusted for the soy sauce and sesame oil dressing. The input values are internally consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for non-starchy mixed vegetables.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Chinese cold salad dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; sesame oil is the primary fat source and is predominantly unsaturated. 2. water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this composite dish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally absent. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated oils are used in this preparation; sesame oil and soy sauce do not contain trans fats. 4. The 'Noodle' ethnic tag in the input record has been removed as it is incorrect — Lao Hu Cai is a vegetable salad dish, not a noodle dish. 5. Potassium 220 mg/100 g is an estimated value derived from USDA FoodData Central data for raw capsicum (~211 mg/100 g) and cucumber (~147 mg/100 g) in approximate proportions; this is documented as an estimate. 6. Source URL https://foodo.sg/ in the input record does not correspond to the official HPB Singapore nutrient database; the approved HPB source has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient reference for this record.
